paper_title: Flexible statistical inference for mechanistic models of neural dynamics
paper_abstract:
Mechanistic models of single-neuron dynamics have been extensively studied in
computational neuroscience. However, identifying which models can
quantitatively reproduce empirically measured data has been challenging. We
propose to overcome this limitation by using likelihood-free inference
approaches (also known as Approximate Bayesian Computation, ABC) to perform
full Bayesian inference on single-neuron models. Our approach builds on recent
advances in ABC by learning a neural network which maps features of the
observed data to the posterior distribution over parameters. We learn a
Bayesian mixture-density network approximating the posterior over multiple
rounds of adaptively chosen simulations. Furthermore, we propose an efficient
approach for handling missing features and parameter settings for which the
simulator fails, as well as a strategy for automatically learning relevant
features using recurrent neural networks. On synthetic data, our approach
efficiently estimates posterior distributions and recovers ground-truth
parameters. On in-vitro recordings of membrane voltages, we recover
multivariate posteriors over biophysical parameters, which yield
model-predicted voltage traces that accurately match empirical data. Our
approach will enable neuroscientists to perform Bayesian inference on complex
neuron models without having to design model-specific algorithms, closing the
gap between mechanistic and statistical approaches to single-neuron modelling.
paper_references: Neuronal dynamics From single neurons to networks and models of cognition

Estimating parameters and predicting membrane voltages with conductance based neuron models
Fitting neuron models to spike trains
Bluepyopt Leveraging open source software and cloud infrastructure to optimise model parameters in neuroscience
Alternative to hand tuning conductance based models Construction and analysis of databases of model neurons
Inhibitory control of correlated intrinsic variability in cortical networks
An efficient automated parameter tuning framework for spiking neural networks
A flexible interactive software tool for fitting the parameters of neuronal models
Monte carlo methods of inference for implicit statistical models
Statistical inference for stochastic simulation models theory and application
Fundamentals and recent developments in approximate bayesian computation
Hodgkin huxley revisited reparametrization and identifiability analysis of the classic action potential model with approximate bayesian methods
Non linear regression models for approximate bayesian computation
Fast epsilon free inference of simulation models with bayesian conditional density estimation
The NEURON Book
Gps abc Gaussian process surrogate approximate bayesian computation
Population growth of human y chromosomes a study of y chromosome microsatellites
Markov chain monte carlo without likelihoods
Hamiltonian abc
Adaptive approximate bayesian computation
Sequential monte carlo with adaptive weights for approximate bayesian computation
Accelerating abc methods using gaussian processes
Statistical inference for noisy nonlinear ecological dynamic systems
Variational bayes with synthetic likelihood
Approximate bayesian computation via regression density estimation
A generalized likelihood free method for posterior estimation
Bayesian synthetic likelihood
Approximate bayesian computation in population genetics
Keeping the neural networks simple by minimizing the description length of the weights
Practical variational inference for neural networks

On the stability and dynamics of stochastic spiking neuron models Nonlinear hawkes process and point process glms
Learning phrase representations using rnn encoder decoder for statistical machine translation
A comparative review of dimension reduction methods in approximate bayesian computation
Learning summary statistic for approximate bayesian computation via deep neural network
Spatio temporal correlations and visual signalling in a complete neuronal population
Bayesian inference for logistic models using p lya gamma latent variables
Bayesian latent structure discovery from multi neuron recordings

Nonparametric input estimation in physiological systems problems methods and case studies
findings: Robustness and flexibility of importance-weighted DELFI: Empirical demonstrations on toy mixture models, Generalized Linear Models, Hodgkin–Huxley neurons, and autapse models show that DELFI is numerically stable across multiple rounds, can capture multi-modal and heavy-tailed posteriors, and outperforms prior ABC methods: Papamakarios & Murray’s SNPE becomes unstable for non-Gaussian proposals, and SMC-ABC requires orders of magnitude more simulations to approach similar accuracy. DELFI also matches likelihood-based samplers (e.g., PG-MCMC) on near-Gaussian posteriors.

Efficient handling of simulator failures and missing outputs: Combining a classifier to predict and reject parameter settings leading to broken or diverging simulations with a learnable imputation layer for undefined features significantly reduces wasted simulation time and allows full Bayesian inference on mechanistic neural models even when large regions of parameter space produce invalid or partially missing outputs.

code_overview:
# Overview of mackelab_delfi (DELFI)

## General Information
- **Name**: DELFI (Density Estimation Likelihood-Free Inference)
- **Type**: Python toolbox for simulation-based inference
- **Status**: Active development (though succeeded by `sbi` project)
- **Documentation**: Available at http://www.mackelab.org/delfi/
- **Build Status**: [![Build Status](https://travis-ci.org/mackelab/delfi.svg?branch=master)](https://travis-ci.org/mackelab/delfi)
- **Successor**: `sbi` (PyTorch-based implementation)

## System Architecture

### Core Components
1. **Distributions Module** (`delfi/distribution/`)
   - Base classes for probability distributions
   - Implementations of various distributions (Gaussian, Poisson, Uniform, etc.)
   - Mixture models (Gaussian Mixture, Student's T Mixture, etc.)
   - Transformed distributions

2. **Generator Module** (`delfi/generator/`)
   - Base generator class
   - Various generator implementations (Default, MPGenerator, RejKernel, RemoteGenerator)
   - Handles parameter and data generation

3. **Inference Module** (`delfi/inference/`)
   - Base inference class
   - Implementations of inference algorithms:
     - APT (Automatic Posterior Transformation)
     - Basic inference
     - SNPEA/B (Sequential Neural Posterior Estimation algorithms)

4. **Neural Network Module** (`delfi/neuralnet/`)
   - Neural network implementation
   - Trainer class
   - Specialized layers for mixture models
   - Loss functions and regularizers

5. **Simulator Module** (`delfi/simulator/`)
   - Base simulator class
   - Various simulator implementations (Gauss, TwoMoons, Blob, etc.)
   - Noise and transformation utilities

6. **Summary Statistics Module** (`delfi/summarystats/`)
   - Base class for summary statistics
   - Implementations (Identity, Mean, MaskedSummaryStats)

7. **Utilities Module** (`delfi/utils/`)
   - Various helper functions:
     - Flow utilities
     - Math operations
     - Visualization tools
     - Data handling
     - Symbolic computation

### Supporting Components
- **Documentation**: Comprehensive docs with tutorials and API references
- **Tests**: Unit and functional tests
- **Build System**: setup.py, requirements.txt, and setup.cfg

## Core Features

### Inference Algorithms
1. **Sequential Neural Posterior Estimation (SNPE)**:
   - SNPE-A and SNPE-B variants
   - Automatic Posterior Transformation (APT)

2. **Density Estimation**:
   - Support for various probability distributions
   - Mixture models for complex distributions
   - Transformed distributions for non-standard spaces

3. **Simulation-Based Inference**:
   - Tools for working with simulators
   - Noise and transformation handling
   - Summary statistics computation

4. **Neural Network Integration**:
   - Custom neural network architecture
   - Specialized layers for mixture models
   - Training utilities

5. **Visualization and Analysis**:
   - Built-in visualization tools
   - Statistical analysis utilities
   - Diagnostic tools for inference quality

### Key Strengths
- Modular design allowing flexible combination of components
- Support for complex probabilistic models
- Comprehensive documentation and tutorials
- Built-in visualization and diagnostic tools
- Active development and testing

## Project Structure
The project follows a well-organized Python package structure with:
- Clear separation of concerns (distributions, inference, neural nets, etc.)
- Comprehensive test suite
- Good documentation coverage
- Modular design allowing for easy extension

Note: This project has been succeeded by `sbi` (https://github.com/mackelab/sbi), which is a PyTorch-based implementation of similar concepts.

techniques.name: Density Estimation Likelihood-Free Inference (DELFI)
techniques.description: An end-to-end likelihood-free Bayesian inference framework for simulator-based mechanistic models of neural dynamics. DELFI approximates the posterior p(θ|x₀) by sequentially training a Bayesian mixture-density network q_φ(θ|x) on simulated data (θ_n, x_n). Each round uses an importance-weighted log-loss to correct for sampling from a proposal distribution ~tilde p(θ) rather than the prior p(θ), and a calibration kernel K(x_n,x₀) to focus learning near the observed data x₀. The proposal for round r+1 is set to the approximate posterior from round r. To exploit all simulations without storing them, DELFI performs stochastic variational inference on the network weights w, representing q(w) as independent Gaussians and penalizing deviations from the previous round’s posterior via a KL term. DELFI also incorporates (1) a learned classifier that predicts and rejects parameter settings leading to broken or diverging simulations, thereby modifying the effective prior, (2) a missing-feature imputation layer with learnable constants for features undefined in some simulations, and (3) an optional recurrent MDN (R-MDN) that learns summary features directly from raw time-series inputs.

code.implementation:
import numpy as np
import scipy.stats as stats
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
from typing import Callable, List, Optional, Tuple, Dict


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Distribution abstractions and sequential proposal adaptation
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
class Distribution(ABC):
    """Abstract base class for univariate probability distributions."""

    @abstractmethod
    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Draw samples from the distribution.

        Parameters
        ----------
        n_samples : int
            Number of samples to draw.

        Returns
        -------
        np.ndarray
            Array of shape (n_samples,) of drawn samples.
        """
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def logpdf(self, x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Compute log-density at given points.

        Parameters
        ----------
        x : np.ndarray
            Points to evaluate, shape (n_samples,).

        Returns
        -------
        np.ndarray
            Log-density values, shape (n_samples,).
        """
        pass

    def convert_to_student_t(self, dofs: int) -> 'Distribution':
        """
        Convert a Gaussian-like distribution to Student's T with same mean/scale.

        Parameters
        ----------
        dofs : int
            Degrees of freedom of the Student's T.

        Returns
        -------
        Distribution
            A StudentTDistribution instance.
        """
        raise NotImplementedError("This distribution does not support Student-T conversion.")


class NormalDistribution(Distribution):
    """Univariate Gaussian distribution."""

    def __init__(self, mean: float, std: float):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        mean : float
            Mean of the Gaussian.
        std : float
            Standard deviation of the Gaussian.
        """
        self.mean = mean
        self.std = std

    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        return np.random.normal(loc=self.mean, scale=self.std, size=n_samples)

    def logpdf(self, x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        return stats.norm.logpdf(x, loc=self.mean, scale=self.std)

    def convert_to_student_t(self, dofs: int) -> 'Distribution':
        return StudentTDistribution(df=dofs, loc=self.mean, scale=self.std)


class StudentTDistribution(Distribution):
    """Univariate Student's T distribution."""

    def __init__(self, df: int, loc: float, scale: float):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        df : int
            Degrees of freedom.
        loc : float
            Location (mean).
        scale : float
            Scale parameter.
        """
        self.df = df
        self.loc = loc
        self.scale = scale

    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        return stats.t.rvs(df=self.df, loc=self.loc, scale=self.scale, size=n_samples)

    def logpdf(self, x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        return stats.t.logpdf(x, df=self.df, loc=self.loc, scale=self.scale)


class SequentialProposalAdaption:
    """
    Sequential proposal adaptation:
    At each round, infer an approximate posterior given current proposal
    and observed data, optionally convert it to Student's T, and use it
    as next-round proposal.
    """

    def __init__(self,
                 prior: Distribution,
                 infer: Callable[[Distribution, np.ndarray], Distribution],
                 n_rounds: int,
                 convert_to_t: Optional[int] = None):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        prior : Distribution
            Initial prior over parameters.
        infer : Callable[[Distribution, np.ndarray], Distribution]
            Inference function: (proposal, obs) -> posterior Distribution.
        n_rounds : int
            Number of adaptation rounds.
        convert_to_t : Optional[int]
            If set, degrees of freedom to convert each posterior to Student's T.
        """
        self.prior = prior
        self.infer = infer
        self.n_rounds = n_rounds
        self.convert_to_t = convert_to_t

    def run(self, obs: np.ndarray) -> List[Distribution]:
        """
        Execute sequential adaptation.

        Parameters
        ----------
        obs : np.ndarray
            Observed data features (1D array).

        Returns
        -------
        List[Distribution]
            Posterior distributions from each round.
        """
        proposal = self.prior
        posteriors: List[Distribution] = []
        for r in range(1, self.n_rounds + 1):
            posterior = self.infer(proposal, obs)
            if self.convert_to_t is not None:
                posterior = posterior.convert_to_student_t(self.convert_to_t)
            posteriors.append(posterior)
            proposal = posterior
        return posteriors


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Continual-learning SVI loss for Bayesian mixture-density networks
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
def svi_kl_zero(means: np.ndarray,
                stds: np.ndarray,
                reg_lambda: float
               ) -> Tuple[float, Dict[str, float]]:
    """
    KL divergence between factorized Gaussian q(w) and zero-mean prior N(0, 1/reg_lambda).

    Parameters
    ----------
    means : np.ndarray, shape (n_params,)
    stds : np.ndarray, shape (n_params,)
    reg_lambda : float
        Prior precision (inverse variance).

    Returns
    -------
    kl : float
        Sum of elementwise KL contributions.
    imvs : dict
        Intermediate monitoring values.
    """
    sigma_p = np.sqrt(1.0 / reg_lambda)
    var_p = sigma_p ** 2
    elem = np.log(sigma_p / stds) + (stds**2 + means**2) / (2 * var_p) - 0.5
    kl = float(np.sum(elem))
    imvs = {
        'sum_log_sigma_ratio': float(np.sum(np.log(sigma_p / stds))),
        'sum_var_term': float(np.sum((stds**2 + means**2) / (2 * var_p))),
        'n_params': means.size
    }
    return kl, imvs


def svi_kl_init(means: np.ndarray,
                stds: np.ndarray,
                prev_means: np.ndarray,
                prev_stds: np.ndarray
               ) -> Tuple[float, Dict[str, float]]:
    """
    KL divergence KL[ q || q_prev ] for two factorized Gaussians.

    Parameters
    ----------
    means : np.ndarray, shape (n_params,)
    stds : np.ndarray, shape (n_params,)
    prev_means : np.ndarray, shape (n_params,)
    prev_stds : np.ndarray, shape (n_params,)

    Returns
    -------
    kl : float
    imvs : dict
    """
    var_prev = prev_stds**2
    elem = (
        np.log(prev_stds / stds)
        + (stds**2 + (means - prev_means)**2) / (2 * var_prev)
        - 0.5
    )
    kl = float(np.sum(elem))
    imvs = {
        'sum_log_sigma_ratio': float(np.sum(np.log(prev_stds / stds))),
        'sum_var_ratio': float(np.sum((stds**2 + (means - prev_means)**2) / (2 * var_prev))),
        'n_params': means.size
    }
    return kl, imvs


class ContinualSVILoss:
    """
    Continual-learning SVI loss:

    L = -E_iw[ log q_w(theta | x) ] + (1/N) KL_term
    where KL_term is between current q and zero-prior (round 1) or previous q (round>1).
    """

    def __init__(self, reg_lambda: float = 0.0):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        reg_lambda : float
            Precision for zero-centered prior (used in round 1).
        """
        self.reg_lambda = reg_lambda
        self.prev_means: Optional[np.ndarray] = None
        self.prev_stds: Optional[np.ndarray] = None

    def loss(self,
             log_probs: np.ndarray,
             iws: np.ndarray,
             means: np.ndarray,
             stds: np.ndarray
            ) -> Tuple[float, Dict[str, float]]:
        """
        Compute loss for current round.

        Parameters
        ----------
        log_probs : np.ndarray, shape (N,)
            Log probability entries log q_w(theta_n|x_n).
        iws : np.ndarray, shape (N,)
            Importance weights, normalized to mean=1.
        means : np.ndarray, shape (n_params,)
            Variational posterior means.
        stds : np.ndarray, shape (n_params,)
            Variational posterior stds.

        Returns
        -------
        loss_value : float
        details : dict
            Contains 'data_term', 'kl_term', and KL monitors.
        """
        N = log_probs.shape[0]
        data_term = -np.mean(iws * log_probs)

        # choose KL computation
        if self.prev_means is None:
            if self.reg_lambda > 0:
                kl, imvs = svi_kl_zero(means, stds, self.reg_lambda)
            else:
                kl, imvs = 0.0, {}
        else:
            kl, imvs = svi_kl_init(means, stds, self.prev_means, self.prev_stds)

        kl_term = kl / float(N)
        loss_value = data_term + kl_term
        details = {'data_term': data_term, 'kl_term': kl_term}
        details.update(imvs)
        return loss_value, details

    def update_previous(self, means: np.ndarray, stds: np.ndarray) -> None:
        """
        Store current variational posterior for next-round KL.

        Parameters
        ----------
        means : np.ndarray
        stds : np.ndarray
        """
        self.prev_means = means.copy()
        self.prev_stds = stds.copy()


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Classifier-based rejection sampler
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
class Prior(ABC):
    """Abstract prior interface."""

    @abstractmethod
    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Draw parameter vectors from prior.

        Returns
        -------
        np.ndarray, shape (n_samples, d_theta)
        """
        pass


class UniformPrior(Prior):
    """Uniform prior on a hyper-rectangle in R^d."""

    def __init__(self, low: np.ndarray, high: np.ndarray, rng: Optional[np.random.Generator] = None):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        low : np.ndarray, shape (d_theta,)
        high : np.ndarray, shape (d_theta,)
        rng : Optional[np.random.Generator]
        """
        self.low = np.array(low)
        self.high = np.array(high)
        self.rng = rng or np.random.default_rng()

    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        d = self.low.size
        return self.rng.uniform(self.low, self.high, size=(n_samples, d))


class ClassifierRejectionSampler:
    """
    Reject parameter proposals based on a classifier g(θ) giving P(reject).
    """

    def __init__(self,
                 prior: Prior,
                 classifier: Callable[[np.ndarray], np.ndarray],
                 rng: Optional[np.random.Generator] = None):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        prior : Prior
        classifier : Callable[[np.ndarray], np.ndarray]
            Maps proposals (batch, d_theta) to rejection probabilities.
        rng : Optional[np.random.Generator]
        """
        self.prior = prior
        self.classifier = classifier
        self.rng = rng or np.random.default_rng()

    def sample(self, n_samples: int, batch_size: int = 1000) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Draw n_samples accepted proposals.

        Parameters
        ----------
        n_samples : int
        batch_size : int

        Returns
        -------
        np.ndarray, shape (n_samples, d_theta)
        """
        accepted = []
        while len(accepted) < n_samples:
            props = self.prior.sample(batch_size)
            p_rej = self.classifier(props)
            if p_rej.ndim != 1 or p_rej.shape[0] != batch_size:
                raise ValueError("Classifier must return 1D array length=batch_size.")
            u = self.rng.random(batch_size)
            mask = u >= p_rej
            for x in props[mask]:
                accepted.append(x)
                if len(accepted) >= n_samples:
                    break
        return np.stack(accepted, axis=0)


# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
code.test:
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # 1) Sequential proposal adaptation with dummy infer
    def dummy_infer(pr: Distribution, obs: np.ndarray) -> Distribution:
        # Posterior mean = obs.mean(), std = prior.std * 0.5
        if isinstance(pr, NormalDistribution):
            m = float(obs.mean())
            s = pr.std * 0.5
            return NormalDistribution(mean=m, std=s)
        return pr

    prior = NormalDistribution(mean=0.0, std=2.0)
    obs = np.array([1.5, 2.0, 1.8])
    spa = SequentialProposalAdaption(prior, dummy_infer, n_rounds=3, convert_to_t=4)
    posts = spa.run(obs)
    print("Sequential posteriors:")
    for i, p in enumerate(posts, 1):
        if isinstance(p, (NormalDistribution, StudentTDistribution)):
            print(f" Round {i}: mean={getattr(p, 'mean', p.loc):.3f}, "
                  f"scale={getattr(p, 'std', p.scale):.3f}")

    # 2) Continual SVI loss demonstration
    N = 100
    log_probs = np.random.randn(N)
    iws = np.ones(N)
    means = np.zeros(5)
    stds = np.ones(5) * 0.2
    svi = ContinualSVILoss(reg_lambda=1.0)
    loss1, details1 = svi.loss(log_probs, iws, means, stds)
    svi.update_previous(means, stds)
    # Next round with shifted means
    means2 = np.ones(5) * 0.1
    stds2 = np.ones(5) * 0.3
    loss2, details2 = svi.loss(log_probs, iws, means2, stds2)
    print("\nSVI losses:")
    print(" Round 1 loss:", loss1, details1)
    print(" Round 2 loss:", loss2, details2)

    # 3) Classifier-based rejection sampling
    up = UniformPrior(low=np.array([-1, -1]), high=np.array([1, 1]))
    # Reject outside unit circle
    def clsf(theta: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        # P(reject) = 1 if outside x^2+y^2>1 else 0
        norm2 = np.sum(theta**2, axis=1)
        return np.where(norm2 > 1.0, 1.0, 0.0)

    rej = ClassifierRejectionSampler(up, clsf)
    samples = rej.sample(5, batch_size=20)
    print("\nAccepted samples (inside unit circle):")
    print(samples)
code.documentation:
This module implements core sub-components of DELFI (Density Estimation Likelihood‐Free Inference):
1) A Distribution abstraction with Normal and Student's T variants, and sequential proposal adaptation that updates proposals to the inferred posterior.
2) A continual‐learning SVI loss (ContinualSVILoss) with closed‐form KL penalties between variational posteriors across rounds (or against a zero‐centered prior).
3) A classifier‐based rejection sampler that filters invalid parameter proposals before simulation. 
Each class and function includes type hints and comprehensive docstrings. 
The TEST BLOCK demonstrates (a) sequential adaptation with a dummy inference function, (b) continual SVI loss calculations across rounds, and (c) classifier‐based sampling on a 2D uniform prior.

components.name: Importance-weighted log-loss for posterior estimation
components.description:
A loss function to train the mixture-density network q_φ(θ|x) directly on samples from a proposal prior ~tilde p(θ). Given N simulated pairs (θ_n, x_n), the loss is

  L(φ) = − (1/N) ∑_{n=1}^N [p(θ_n)/~tilde p(θ_n)] K(x_n, x₀) log q_φ(θ_n|x_n)

where K(x_n, x₀) ≥ 0 is a calibration kernel (often 1 or used to reject bad simulations). This estimator converges to minimizing KL divergence between the true posterior p(θ|x) (restricted by K) and q_φ, without any closed-form division by the proposal.

components.name: Sequential proposal adaptation
components.description: An adaptive sampling scheme in which the proposal prior for round r ( ~tilde p^{(r)}(θ) ) is initialized to the user prior p(θ) and then updated to the approximate posterior q_{φ^{(r−1)}}(θ|x₀) from the previous round. This focuses new simulations on high-posterior regions, improving efficiency over naive prior sampling.

code.implementation:
import numpy as np
from typing import Callable, List, Optional
from abc import ABC, abstractmethod
import scipy.stats as stats


class Distribution(ABC):
    """Abstract base class for probability distributions."""

    @abstractmethod
    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Generate samples from the distribution.

        Parameters:
        -----------
        n_samples: int
            Number of samples to draw.

        Returns:
        --------
        samples: np.ndarray
            Array of drawn samples.
        """
        pass

    @abstractmethod
    def logpdf(self, x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Compute the log-density of given samples.

        Parameters:
        -----------
        x: np.ndarray
            Points at which to evaluate log-density.

        Returns:
        --------
        log_probs: np.ndarray
            Log-density values.
        """
        pass

    def convert_to_student_t(self, dofs: int) -> 'Distribution':
        """
        Convert a Gaussian-style distribution to a Student's T distribution
        with the same location and scale parameters.

        Parameters:
        -----------
        dofs: int
            Degrees of freedom for the Student's T distribution.

        Returns:
        --------
        Distribution
            A StudentTDistribution instance.
        """
        raise NotImplementedError("This distribution does not support Student-T conversion.")


class NormalDistribution(Distribution):
    """Univariate Gaussian distribution."""

    def __init__(self, mean: float, std: float):
        """
        Parameters:
        -----------
        mean: float
            Mean of the Gaussian.
        std: float
            Standard deviation of the Gaussian.
        """
        self.mean = mean
        self.std = std

    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        return np.random.normal(self.mean, self.std, size=n_samples)

    def logpdf(self, x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        return stats.norm.logpdf(x, loc=self.mean, scale=self.std)

    def convert_to_student_t(self, dofs: int) -> 'Distribution':
        return StudentTDistribution(df=dofs, loc=self.mean, scale=self.std)


class StudentTDistribution(Distribution):
    """Univariate Student's T distribution."""

    def __init__(self, df: int, loc: float, scale: float):
        """
        Parameters:
        -----------
        df: int
            Degrees of freedom.
        loc: float
            Location parameter (mean).
        scale: float
            Scale parameter.
        """
        self.df = df
        self.loc = loc
        self.scale = scale

    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        return stats.t.rvs(df=self.df, loc=self.loc, scale=self.scale, size=n_samples)

    def logpdf(self, x: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        return stats.t.logpdf(x, df=self.df, loc=self.loc, scale=self.scale)


class SequentialProposalAdaption:
    """
    Implements sequential proposal adaptation where the proposal distribution
    for each round is set to the approximate posterior from the previous round.
    """

    def __init__(self,
                 prior: Distribution,
                 infer: Callable[[Distribution, np.ndarray], Distribution],
                 n_rounds: int,
                 convert_to_t: Optional[int] = None):
        """
        Parameters:
        -----------
        prior: Distribution
            The initial prior distribution over parameters.
        infer: Callable[[Distribution, np.ndarray], Distribution]
            A user-supplied inference function that takes the current proposal
            and observed data and returns an approximate posterior distribution.
        n_rounds: int
            Number of adaptation rounds.
        convert_to_t: Optional[int]
            If provided, degrees of freedom to convert each posterior into a
            Student's T distribution for the next proposal.
        """
        self.prior = prior
        self.infer = infer
        self.n_rounds = n_rounds
        self.convert_to_t = convert_to_t

    def run(self, obs: np.ndarray) -> List[Distribution]:
        """
        Run the sequential proposal adaptation for a fixed number of rounds.

        Parameters:
        -----------
        obs: np.ndarray
            Observed data used by the inference function.

        Returns:
        --------
        posteriors: List[Distribution]
            The approximate posterior distributions obtained in each round.
        """
        proposal = self.prior
        posteriors: List[Distribution] = []

        for round_idx in range(1, self.n_rounds + 1):
            # Obtain approximate posterior given current proposal and data
            posterior = self.infer(proposal, obs)

            # Optionally convert posterior to Student's T for robust proposals
            if self.convert_to_t is not None:
                dofs = self.convert_to_t if isinstance(self.convert_to_t, int) else 10
                posterior = posterior.convert_to_student_t(dofs)

            # Update proposal for next round
            proposal = posterior
            posteriors.append(posterior)

        return posteriors
code.test:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # A dummy inference function that creates a Normal posterior centered at the
    # observed data mean with reduced variance.
    def dummy_infer(proposal: Distribution, obs: np.ndarray) -> Distribution:
        samples = proposal.sample(500)
        # Posterior mean as observed data mean; posterior std shrinks with sqrt(n)
        post_mean = float(np.mean(obs))
        post_std = float(np.std(samples) / np.sqrt(len(samples)))
        return NormalDistribution(mean=post_mean, std=post_std)

    # Set up prior, observed data, and adaptor
    prior_dist = NormalDistribution(mean=0.0, std=1.0)
    observed_data = np.array([2.0, 2.5, 3.1, 1.8, 2.9])
    adaptor = SequentialProposalAdaption(
        prior=prior_dist,
        infer=dummy_infer,
        n_rounds=4,
        convert_to_t=5
    )

    # Run sequential adaptation and display results
    posteriors = adaptor.run(observed_data)
    for idx, post in enumerate(posteriors, start=1):
        mean_attr = getattr(post, 'mean', None)
        std_attr = getattr(post, 'std', None)
        df_attr = getattr(post, 'df', None)
        print(f"Round {idx}: mean={mean_attr:.3f}, std={std_attr:.3f}, df={df_attr}")
code.documentation:
This module implements Sequential Proposal Adaptation, an adaptive scheme
where the proposal distribution for each round is updated to the approximate
posterior obtained from the previous round. It defines an abstract Distribution
interface and concrete Normal and Student's T distributions with sampling and
log-pdf evaluation. The SequentialProposalAdaption class orchestrates the
adaptation over a fixed number of rounds by calling a user-supplied inference
function, and optionally converts each posterior into a Student's T form for
robustness. In the TEST BLOCK, a dummy inference function demonstrates how to
plug in a simple Bayesian update, and the sequential rounds are printed out
with their updated distribution parameters.

components.name: Bayesian mixture-density network with continual learning
components.description:
A training procedure for the mixture-density network q_w(θ|x) that treats its weights w as random variables with a variational posterior q_{φ}(w) = ∏ N(w_i | μ_i, σ_i²). At each round r, stochastic variational inference minimizes

  L(φ^{(r)}) = − (1/N) ∑_n [p(θ_n)/~tilde p^{(r)}(θ_n)] K(x_n, x₀) ⟨log q_w(θ_n|x_n)⟩_{q_{φ^{(r)}}(w)}
            + (1/N) KL[q_{φ^{(r)}}(w) || q_{φ^{(r−1)}}(w)]

The KL term uses the round-(r−1) posterior as the prior for round r, penalizing large weight shifts for well-constrained parameters and enabling continual learning without storing past samples.

code.implementation:
import numpy as np
from typing import Tuple, Dict, Optional


def svi_kl_zero(means: np.ndarray,
                stds: np.ndarray,
                reg_lambda: float
               ) -> Tuple[float, Dict[str, float]]:
    """
    Compute the KL divergence between the variational posterior q(w) = ∏ N(means_i, stds_i^2)
    and a zero-centered Gaussian prior p(w) with precision reg_lambda (variance = 1/reg_lambda).

    Parameters
    ----------
    means : np.ndarray
        Array of posterior means (shape: [n_params]).
    stds : np.ndarray
        Array of posterior standard deviations (shape: [n_params]).
    reg_lambda : float
        Prior precision (i.e., inverse variance) for the zero-centered Gaussian prior.

    Returns
    -------
    kl : float
        The total KL divergence summed over all parameters.
    imvs : Dict[str, float]
        Dictionary of intermediate values for monitoring:
            'sum_log_sigma_ratio' : sum_i log(sigma_prior / stds_i)
            'sum_var_term'        : sum_i (stds_i^2 + means_i^2) / (2 * var_prior)
            'n_params'            : number of parameters
    """
    # Prior standard deviation
    sigma_p = np.sqrt(1.0 / reg_lambda)
    var_p = sigma_p ** 2

    # Element-wise KL contributions
    kl_elems = (
        np.log(sigma_p / stds)
        + (stds**2 + means**2) / (2.0 * var_p)
        - 0.5
    )
    kl = np.sum(kl_elems)

    imvs = {
        'sum_log_sigma_ratio': float(np.sum(np.log(sigma_p / stds))),
        'sum_var_term': float(np.sum((stds**2 + means**2) / (2.0 * var_p))),
        'n_params': means.size
    }
    return kl, imvs


def svi_kl_init(means: np.ndarray,
                stds: np.ndarray,
                prev_means: np.ndarray,
                prev_stds: np.ndarray
               ) -> Tuple[float, Dict[str, float]]:
    """
    Compute the KL divergence between the current variational posterior q(w)
    and the previous round's variational posterior p_prev(w).

    KL(q||p_prev) where both distributions are factorized Gaussians:
      q_i = N(means_i, stds_i^2),  p_prev_i = N(prev_means_i, prev_stds_i^2).

    Parameters
    ----------
    means : np.ndarray
        Array of current posterior means (shape: [n_params]).
    stds : np.ndarray
        Array of current posterior standard deviations (shape: [n_params]).
    prev_means : np.ndarray
        Array of previous posterior means (shape: [n_params]).
    prev_stds : np.ndarray
        Array of previous posterior standard deviations (shape: [n_params]).

    Returns
    -------
    kl : float
        Total KL divergence summed over all parameters.
    imvs : Dict[str, float]
        Dictionary of intermediate values for monitoring:
            'sum_log_sigma_ratio' : sum_i log(prev_stds_i / stds_i)
            'sum_var_ratio'       : sum_i (stds_i^2 + (means_i - prev_means_i)^2) / (2 * prev_stds_i^2)
            'n_params'            : number of parameters
    """
    var_prev = prev_stds ** 2

    kl_elems = (
        np.log(prev_stds / stds)
        + (stds**2 + (means - prev_means)**2) / (2.0 * var_prev)
        - 0.5
    )
    kl = np.sum(kl_elems)

    imvs = {
        'sum_log_sigma_ratio': float(np.sum(np.log(prev_stds / stds))),
        'sum_var_ratio': float(np.sum((stds**2 + (means - prev_means)**2) / (2.0 * var_prev))),
        'n_params': means.size
    }
    return kl, imvs


class ContinualSVILoss:
    """
    Implements the continual-learning SVI loss:
      L = - (1/N) sum_n [iws_n * log_probs_n]
          + (1/N) KL[q_phi^(r)(w) || q_phi^(r-1)(w)]

    Where:
    - log_probs_n are log q_w(theta_n | x_n) evaluated at different samples.
    - iws_n are importance weights [p(theta_n)/tilde p^(r)(theta_n)] * K(x_n, x0),
      normalized to have mean 1.
    - At round r=1 the KL is computed against a zero-centered prior with precision reg_lambda.
    - For r>1 the KL is computed against the previous round's posterior.
    """

    def __init__(self, reg_lambda: float = 0.0):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        reg_lambda : float
            Precision (inverse variance) of the zero-centered prior used in round 1.
            If zero or negative, no prior regularization is applied in round 1.
        """
        self.reg_lambda = reg_lambda
        self.prev_means: Optional[np.ndarray] = None
        self.prev_stds: Optional[np.ndarray] = None

    def loss(self,
             log_probs: np.ndarray,
             iws: np.ndarray,
             means: np.ndarray,
             stds: np.ndarray
            ) -> Tuple[float, Dict[str, float]]:
        """
        Compute the continual-SVI loss for the current round.

        Parameters
        ----------
        log_probs : np.ndarray
            Array of log q_w(theta_n | x_n), shape (N,).
        iws : np.ndarray
            Array of importance weights for each sample, shape (N,). Should be normalized (mean 1).
        means : np.ndarray
            Current variational posterior means for all network weights, shape (n_params,).
        stds : np.ndarray
            Current variational posterior standard deviations, shape (n_params,).

        Returns
        -------
        loss_value : float
            The scalar value of the loss L.
        details : Dict[str, float]
            Dictionary containing:
              'data_term': The - (1/N) sum_n iws_n * log_probs_n term,
              'kl_term': The (1/N) * KL divergence,
              plus any intermediate monitoring values from the KL computation.
        """
        N = log_probs.shape[0]
        # Data-fitting term
        data_term = - np.mean(iws * log_probs)

        # KL term
        if self.prev_means is None:
            # First round: compare to zero-centered prior if lambda>0
            if self.reg_lambda > 0:
                kl, imvs = svi_kl_zero(means, stds, self.reg_lambda)
            else:
                kl, imvs = 0.0, {}
        else:
            kl, imvs = svi_kl_init(means, stds,
                                    prev_means=self.prev_means,
                                    prev_stds=self.prev_stds)

        kl_term = kl / float(N)
        loss_value = data_term + kl_term

        details = {'data_term': data_term, 'kl_term': kl_term}
        details.update(imvs)
        return loss_value, details

    def update_previous(self,
                        means: np.ndarray,
                        stds: np.ndarray) -> None:
        """
        After completing a round of training, call this to store the current
        variational posterior as the prior for the next round.

        Parameters
        ----------
        means : np.ndarray
            Posterior means from this round.
        stds : np.ndarray
            Posterior standard deviations from this round.
        """
        self.prev_means = means.copy()
        self.prev_stds = stds.copy()
code.test:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Simulate a small example with 2 network weights and 5 samples
    np.random.seed(0)
    N_samples = 5
    # Simulated log-probs and importance weights
    log_probs = np.random.randn(N_samples) - 1.0
    iws = np.ones(N_samples)  # assume already normalized

    # Current variational parameters (2 weights)
    means_round1 = np.array([0.1, -0.2])
    stds_round1 = np.array([0.05, 0.1])

    svi_loss = ContinualSVILoss(reg_lambda=0.5)
    loss1, details1 = svi_loss.loss(log_probs, iws, means_round1, stds_round1)
    print("Round 1 loss:", loss1)
    print("Round 1 details:", details1)

    # Update prior for next round
    svi_loss.update_previous(means_round1, stds_round1)

    # Simulate new posterior parameters for round 2
    means_round2 = np.array([0.15, -0.15])
    stds_round2 = np.array([0.04, 0.08])
    loss2, details2 = svi_loss.loss(log_probs, iws, means_round2, stds_round2)
    print("\nRound 2 loss:", loss2)
    print("Round 2 details:", details2)
code.documentation: This module provides functionality to compute the continual-learning SVI loss for Bayesian mixture-density networks, as described in "Flexible statistical inference for mechanistic models of neural dynamics". The core class, ContinualSVILoss, maintains variational posterior parameters across rounds and adds a KL regularization term between successive posteriors (or against a zero-centered prior in round 1). Functions svi_kl_zero and svi_kl_init compute closed-form KL divergences for factorized Gaussians. Users pass per-sample log-probabilities and importance weights, along with current posterior means/stds, to compute the loss. After each training round, calling update_previous() readies the class for continual learning. The TEST BLOCK demonstrates round-wise loss computation with synthetic data. Continuous parameters are passed with clear type hints, and intermediate monitoring values are returned for debugging or logging.

components.name: Classifier-based proposal rejection for invalid simulations
components.description: A binary neural network ĝ(θ): θ → [0,1] that predicts the probability that a simulator run will break or produce invalid output (e.g., diverging firing rates). During sampling, each proposed θ is rejected with probability ĝ(θ) before simulation. Since this rejection does not depend on x₀, it effectively modifies the prior to exclude regions of parameter space yielding nonsensical simulations.

code.implementation:
import numpy as np
from typing import Callable, Optional

class Prior:
    """
    Abstract prior distribution interface.
    """

    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Draw samples from the prior.

        Parameters
        ----------
        n_samples : int
            Number of samples to draw.

        Returns
        -------
        samples : np.ndarray, shape (n_samples, d_theta)
            Drawn parameter vectors.
        """
        raise NotImplementedError("Prior.sample must be implemented by subclasses.")


class UniformPrior(Prior):
    """
    Uniform prior over a hyper-rectangle in R^d.
    """

    def __init__(self, low: np.ndarray, high: np.ndarray, rng: Optional[np.random.Generator] = None):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        low : np.ndarray, shape (d_theta,)
            Lower bounds for each parameter dimension.
        high : np.ndarray, shape (d_theta,)
            Upper bounds for each parameter dimension.
        rng : np.random.Generator, optional
            Random number generator for reproducibility.
        """
        self.low = np.array(low)
        self.high = np.array(high)
        self.rng = rng or np.random.default_rng()

    def sample(self, n_samples: int) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Draw uniform samples in the bounding box [low, high].

        Parameters
        ----------
        n_samples : int
            Number of samples to draw.

        Returns
        -------
        samples : np.ndarray, shape (n_samples, d_theta)
            Drawn parameter vectors.
        """
        d = self.low.size
        return self.rng.uniform(self.low, self.high, size=(n_samples, d))


class ClassifierRejectionSampler:
    """
    Sampler that uses a classifier to reject invalid parameter proposals.

    Given a prior distribution over parameters θ and a classifier g_hat(θ)
    that predicts the probability of invalid simulations, this sampler
    draws proposals from the prior and rejects each θ with probability
    g_hat(θ) before simulation. The result is a set of valid parameter
    vectors adapted to the modified prior.
    """

    def __init__(
        self,
        prior: Prior,
        classifier: Callable[[np.ndarray], np.ndarray],
        rng: Optional[np.random.Generator] = None
    ):
        """
        Parameters
        ----------
        prior : Prior
            A Prior instance implementing sample(n_samples) -> θ-array.
        classifier : Callable[[np.ndarray], np.ndarray]
            Function mapping θ-array of shape (batch, d_theta) to
            rejection probabilities in [0, 1], shape (batch,).
        rng : np.random.Generator, optional
            Random number generator for sampling and accept/reject draws.
        """
        self.prior = prior
        self.classifier = classifier
        self.rng = rng or np.random.default_rng()

    def sample(
        self,
        n_samples: int,
        batch_size: int = 1000
    ) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Draw valid parameter samples applying classifier-based rejection.

        Parameters
        ----------
        n_samples : int
            Number of accepted samples required.
        batch_size : int, optional
            Number of proposals to draw per iteration from the prior.

        Returns
        -------
        accepted : np.ndarray, shape (n_samples, d_theta)
            Array of accepted parameter vectors.
        """
        accepted = []
        while len(accepted) < n_samples:
            # Draw a batch of proposals
            proposals = self.prior.sample(batch_size)
            # Compute rejection probabilities for each proposal
            p_reject = self.classifier(proposals)
            if p_reject.ndim != 1 or p_reject.shape[0] != batch_size:
                raise ValueError("Classifier must return 1D array of length batch_size.")
            # Draw uniforms to decide acceptance
            u = self.rng.random(batch_size)
            # Accept proposals where u >= p_reject
            mask = u >= p_reject
            for prop in proposals[mask]:
                accepted.append(prop)
                if len(accepted) >= n_samples:
                    break
        return np.stack(accepted, axis=0)
code.test:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Define a 2D uniform prior between [-5, 5]^2
    low = np.array([-5.0, -5.0])
    high = np.array([5.0, 5.0])
    prior = UniformPrior(low, high, rng=np.random.default_rng(42))

    # Define a toy classifier: higher sum of theta => higher rejection
    def toy_classifier(thetas: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
        """
        Probabilistic classifier returning rejection probability
        based on a sigmoid of the parameter sum.
        """
        s = thetas.sum(axis=1)  # shape (batch,)
        # Sigmoid mapping to (0,1)
        return 1 / (1 + np.exp(-0.5 * (s - 2.0)))

    sampler = ClassifierRejectionSampler(prior, toy_classifier, rng=np.random.default_rng(123))

    # Draw 100 valid samples
    samples = sampler.sample(n_samples=100, batch_size=50)
    print("Drawn samples shape:", samples.shape)
    print("First 5 samples:\n", samples[:5])
code.documentation:
This module implements classifier-based proposal rejection to filter
out parameter vectors likely to yield invalid simulation outputs.
A user provides a Prior object over the parameter space and a
classifier function g_hat(θ) → [0,1] indicating the rejection
probability of each θ. The sampler draws batches of proposals,
evaluates g_hat, and stochastically rejects each candidate before
any forward simulation. This effectively reshapes the prior away
from regions that produce nonsensical or diverging outputs.
Configurable options include the prior distribution, classifier
behavior, and batch size for proposal drawing. The provided
test block demonstrates usage with a toy uniform prior and a
sigmoidal classifier. The code ensures reproducibility via
numpy's Generator and can be extended to any dimension or
classifier model.

components.name: Missing-feature imputation via learnable constants
components.description:
A method to handle simulations where some summary features f_i(s) are undefined (e.g., spike latency when no spikes occur). For each feature index i, introduce a missing indicator m_i(s) ∈ {0,1}. In the MDN input layer, replace f_i with

  h_i = f_i(s)·(1 − m_i(s)) + c_i·m_i(s)

where c_i is a learnable scalar parameter. During MDN training, c_i is optimized alongside network weights, automatically imputing appropriate values for missing features.

code.implementation:
import numpy as np
import lasagne
import lasagne.layers as ll
import theano
import theano.tensor as tt
from typing import Tuple, Sequence, Optional

# Use theano's default float type
dtype = theano.config.floatX

class ImputeMissingLayer(ll.Layer):
    """
    A Lasagne layer that replaces NaN values in the input tensor with
    learnable imputation constants.

    For each feature index i, if input[..., i] is NaN, it is replaced by
    a learned scalar c_i. These scalars are initialized via `R` and are
    optimized during training.

    Parameters
    ----------
    incoming : lasagne.layers.Layer
        The incoming layer providing the input tensor.
    n_inputs : Sequence[int]
        The shape of the last (feature) dimensions for which we want to learn
        an imputation constant. For a 2D input (batch_size, num_features),
        use n_inputs=(num_features,).
    R : lasagne.init.Initializer, optional
        Initializer for the imputation constants c_i. Default is
        lasagne.init.Normal(0.01).
    """
    def __init__(
        self,
        incoming: ll.Layer,
        n_inputs: Sequence[int],
        R: Optional[lasagne.init.Initializer] = lasagne.init.Normal(0.01),
        **kwargs
    ) -> None:
        super(ImputeMissingLayer, self).__init__(incoming, **kwargs)
        # Add a learnable parameter vector of shape n_inputs
        self.imputation_values = self.add_param(
            R, tuple(n_inputs), name='imputation_values'
        )

    def get_output_for(
        self,
        input: tt.TensorVariable,
        **kwargs
    ) -> tt.TensorVariable:
        """
        Replace NaNs in the input tensor with corresponding learned values.

        Parameters
        ----------
        input : theano.tensor.TensorVariable
            Theano tensor of any shape, where NaNs indicate missing entries.

        Returns
        -------
        theano.tensor.TensorVariable
            Tensor of the same shape as `input`, with NaNs replaced by
            the learned imputation constants.
        """
        # Use switch: if NaN, pick imputation value, else keep original
        return tt.cast(tt.switch(tt.isnan(input),
                                 self.imputation_values,
                                 input),
                       dtype)

    def get_output_shape_for(
        self,
        input_shape: Tuple[int, ...]
    ) -> Tuple[int, ...]:
        """
        The output shape is identical to the input shape.

        Parameters
        ----------
        input_shape : tuple of int
            Shape of the input tensor.

        Returns
        -------
        tuple of int
            Same as `input_shape`.
        """
        return input_shape
code.test:
if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Create a Theano symbolic input variable (batch_size x num_features)
    input_var = tt.matrix('inputs', dtype=dtype)

    # Build a small network: InputLayer -> ImputeMissingLayer
    # Here we override R to a constant initializer to make testing deterministic
    n_features = 3
    constant_impute_val = 0.5
    inp_layer = ll.InputLayer(shape=(None, n_features), input_var=input_var)
    impute_layer = ImputeMissingLayer(
        incoming=inp_layer,
        n_inputs=(n_features,),
        R=lasagne.init.Constant(constant_impute_val)
    )

    # Compile a Theano function to test the layer
    output_expr = ll.get_output(impute_layer)
    impute_fn = theano.function([input_var], output_expr)

    # Sample data with NaNs indicating missing features
    test_data = np.array([
        [1.0, np.nan, 3.0],
        [np.nan, 5.0, np.nan]
    ], dtype=dtype)

    # Apply imputation
    imputed = impute_fn(test_data)
    print("Input array:")
    print(test_data)
    print("\nAfter imputation (NaNs -> 0.5):")
    print(imputed)
code.documentation: This module defines ImputeMissingLayer, a Lasagne layer that automatically imputes missing features (represented as NaNs) with learnable per-feature constants. Upon initialization, you specify the shape of the feature dimension and an initializer for the imputation values. The layer then casts each NaN entry in the input tensor to the corresponding learned constant during forward passes. All constants are optimized together with the network parameters. The TEST BLOCK demonstrates how to integrate this layer in a small network, compile a Theano function, and apply it to sample data containing NaNs, replacing them with a fixed constant for illustrative purposes.

components.name: Recurrent mixture-density network for learned summary features
components.description: An extension that replaces the MDN’s fixed precomputed summary-feature input with a gated recurrent unit (GRU) that processes the full time series of stimulus and observed voltage. A many-to-one RNN with N GRU units unrolled over T time steps produces an N-dimensional feature vector passed to the MDN. By minimizing the same variational objective, the RNN learns task-relevant summary features from raw data, eliminating manual feature engineering.
